  • Portable Operations — portable station as the basis of an EmComm setup
  • Safety — safe setup in crisis conditions

docs.title: Emergency Procedures
docs.text:
In emergency communications, discipline saves lives. The difference between chaotic and coordinated radio communications in a disaster situation can be decisive for the timely response of rescue services.

This page covers the fundamental principles, standard message formats, and rules of conduct on an EmComm net.

docs.title: 1. Listening is primary
docs.text:
Before you transmit anything — listen. Find out: - Is the frequency already in use? - Is there a Net Control Station (NCS)? - What type of traffic is there — routine, urgent, priority?

Rule: 90% of the time you listen, 10% you transmit.

docs.title: 2. Short, clear, no jargon
docs.text: EmComm communication is not a nuanced conversation — it is the transmission of information under stress. Use: - Standard phrases (not improvisation) - Phonetic alphabet for spelling critical data - Short sentences — one message, one idea - Numbers spoken distinctly — "one-zero-two" not "one hundred and two"

docs.title: 3. Write everything down
docs.text:
Every QSO in EmComm must be documented: - UTC time - Callsign of the sender - Message content - Action taken / forwarded to whom

A paper log is a backup when the laptop fails. Always have a notebook and pen.

docs.title: 4. Do not transmit without NCS permission
docs.text:
When a Net Control Station is operating, all communication goes through the NCS. Do not call other stations directly, do not relay messages without announcing, do not check into the net and immediately start transmitting.

Frequency stepping = chaos

Simultaneous transmission by multiple stations (carrier clash) results in an unintelligible signal and a lost message. In an EmComm situation that means lost information about casualties or locations. Maintain discipline.

docs.title: Message Priority
docs.text:
Messages are classified by urgency:

Priority Name Description Example 🔴 1 DISTRESS / MAYDAY Immediate danger to life "Mayday, mayday — vessel sinking, 12 persons on board" 🟠 2 URGENCY / PAN-PAN Urgent situation, no immediate threat to life "Pan-Pan — passenger with cardiac arrest, need ambulance at location X" 🟡 3 SAFETY / SÉCURITÉ Warning of danger to others "Sécurité — earthquake recorded, possible road damage on route Y" 🟢 4 WELFARE Status information about casualties / injured List of evacuees, infrastructure condition ⚪ 5 ROUTINE Routine coordination and logistics Frequency change, station status, relay request

docs.title: MAYDAY call
docs.text:
Use exclusively for immediate danger to life:

MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY
This is [callsign]
Mayday — [description of situation in 1-2 sentences]
Our position is [coordinates or location description]
There are [number of persons] at this location
We require [food/water/medical aid/evacuation]
[callsign], over

docs.title: PAN-PAN call
docs.text:
For urgent situations without immediate threat to life:

PAN-PAN PAN-PAN PAN-PAN
All stations, this is [callsign]
Pan-Pan — [description of situation]
Location: [description]
[Request for assistance]
[callsign], over

docs.title: IARU Standard EmComm Message
docs.text:
For structured transmission of information through the net (written — Winlink or relay):

1. PRECEDENCE: [Routine / Welfare / Priority / Emergency]
2. FROM: [callsign]
3. TO: [callsign or institution]
4. DATE/TIME (UTC): [DD/MMM/YYYY HH:MM]
5. RECEIVED VIA: [relay station callsigns]
6. MESSAGE:
   [Clear, brief message. One idea per sentence.
    Do not use abbreviations the recipient may not know.]
7. END OF MESSAGE
8. [sender's callsign]

Example IARU EmComm message

1. PRECEDENCE: Priority
2. FROM: 9A7YY
3. TO: 9A1XX (NCS)
4. DATE/TIME: 29/DEC/2024 14:35 UTC
5. RECEIVED VIA: Direct
6. MESSAGE:
   Reporting from location Petrinja, Moslavačka Street.
   Building at number 22 partially collapsed, I can see 3 survivors.
   Access difficult — street blocked by rubble.
   Coordinates: 45.4417 N, 16.2744 E.
   Heavy machinery required for access.
7. END OF MESSAGE
8. 9A7YY

docs.title: Phonetic Alphabet (ICAO/NATO)
docs.text:
In EmComm communication, every letter that could be misheard must be spelled using the phonetic alphabet:

Letter NATO Letter NATO A Alpha N November B Bravo O Oscar C Charlie P Papa D Delta Q Quebec E Echo R Romeo F Foxtrot S Sierra G Golf T Tango H Hotel U Uniform I India V Victor J Juliet W Whiskey K Kilo X X-ray L Lima Y Yankee M Mike Z Zulu

Numbers are spoken clearly: "niner" for 9 (to avoid confusion with German "nein"), "fife" for 5 (clearly distinct from "fire").

docs.title: Special Rules on EmComm Frequencies
docs.text:
• Never test your antenna by transmitting on an EmComm frequency — it interferes with the net
  • Do not use the frequency for routine contacts except when called by NCS
  • If you hear a MAYDAY — stop transmitting, listen, and write down the information
  • Relay responsibility — if you hear a distress call and NCS has not acknowledged it, it is your duty to act

Legal aspect

Rendering assistance to persons in distress is a moral and in some jurisdictions a legal obligation for radio operators. Ignoring a MAYDAY you received without taking action is not acceptable.

docs.title: Training and Preparation
docs.text:
EmComm skills fade without regular practice. Recommended minimum:

  • Weekly: Check in to the local EmComm net — just confirm your presence
  • Monthly: Club EmComm exercise with message traffic
  • Annually: Field Day (deploying a portable station without mains power)

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• RMZO — Croatian EmComm organisation and frequencies
  • Winlink — sending structured messages digitally
  • Portable Operations — portable station

docs.title: RMZO — Croatian Amateur Radio Emergency Network
docs.text:
RMZO (Radioamaterska mreža za opasnost — Amateur Radio Emergency Network) is an organised system within the Croatian Amateur Radio Association that is activated in the event of natural disasters, technical accidents or any situation where standard telecommunications networks are not functioning.

RMZO is not just a list of frequencies — it is an organisational structure that defines who coordinates whom, which frequencies are used, what message priorities apply, and how reports are submitted to the competent authorities.

docs.title: Legal Framework
docs.text:
RMZO operates in cooperation with: - HAKOM — Croatian Regulatory Authority for Network Industries (assigns special EmComm frequencies) - DUZS/MUP Civil Protection Directorate — government civil protection authority - HRS — coordinates and leads RMZO at the national level - 112 — integration with the national emergency call centre

The Electronic Communications Act gives priority to communications involving danger to life and property over all other traffic.

docs.title: Organisational Structure
docs.text:
HRS (national coordination)
        │
        ▼
Regional coordinators (by county)
        │
        ▼
Local radio clubs and RMZO operators
        │
        ▼
Field stations (mobile and portable)

Roles in RMZO:

Role Description Net Control Station (NCS) Manages the net, grants permission to transmit, logs all traffic Relay station Forwards traffic between stations that cannot hear each other directly Field station Reports from the disaster location or coordination centre Base station Fixed RMZO station, often a club shack with backup power

docs.title: RMZO Frequencies
docs.text:
Frequencies may change

Always verify current RMZO frequencies at hamradio.hr as they may be updated by HRS and HAKOM decisions. Keep a current list in your portable station.

docs.title: HF frequencies (long range, no infrastructure)
docs.text: Frequency Band Purpose 3.675 MHz 80 m Primary national EmComm frequency for Croatia 7.060 MHz 40 m Alternative for daytime work (better daytime propagation) 14.300 MHz 20 m IARU Region 1 EmComm frequency (international)

docs.title: VHF/UHF frequencies (local range, clarity)
docs.text: Frequency Band Purpose 145.500 MHz 2 m Primary local simplex EmComm frequency 144.800 MHz 2 m APRS (automatic packet reporting) 433.500 MHz 70 cm UHF simplex working Local repeaters 2 m / 70 cm Coordination if backup power is available

docs.title: How is the network activated?
docs.text:
RMZO can be activated in two ways:

  1. Official request — DUZS/MUP/112 contacts the HRS coordinator and requests activation
  2. Self-initiated — if it is obvious that communications infrastructure has failed (earthquake, flood, widespread power outage), amateur radio operators can self-organise without waiting for a request

In practice, major disasters trigger both mechanisms simultaneously.

docs.title: Activation Phases
docs.text:
Phase 1 — Monitoring: Operators listen on EmComm frequencies and monitor the situation without transmitting unless they have urgent information.

Phase 2 — Net activation: NCS calls the net and takes over coordination. All stations report their location and availability.

Phase 3 — Operational work: Field stations send reports. NCS filters information and forwards it to the competent authorities.

Phase 4 — Deactivation: NCS announces the end of the operation. All traffic is archived.

docs.title: Example Net Opening
docs.text:
NCS: "All stations, all stations — this is 9A1XX, Net Control for the RMZO EmComm net.
      Opening EmComm net on frequency 3.675 MHz at [UTC]. Mode: SSB.
      Stations with urgent traffic — respond immediately.
      All other stations — respond in callsign order when called.
      9A1XX, standing by."

Field station: "9A1XX, this is 9A7YY from Petrinja, I have a Priority report."

NCS: "9A7YY, you have clearance — go ahead with your report."

docs.title: EmComm Go-Bag Contents
docs.text:
A go-bag is a bag or rucksack with equipment ready for rapid field deployment:

Item Notes Dual-band handheld (VHF/UHF) Charged battery + spare HF QRP transceiver If available (IC-705, KX2, FT-818) LiFePO4 battery 10+ Ah Independent power source Solar panel 20–30 W For extended operation Wire antenna (EFHW or dipole) 10–15 m of wire, connector, short mast Laptop/tablet with Winlink Express Digital messaging VARA modem or SignaLink Audio interface for digital modes Log notebook and pens Paper log always as backup EmComm frequency list Laminated, in the bag Power adapters 12 V and 230 V versions

Test your go-bag regularly

Once per quarter: unpack the equipment, verify batteries are charged, make a short QSO as a test. Equipment that hasn't been tested is not reliable equipment.

docs.title: Field Day — Annual Exercise
docs.text:
Field Day is an annual EmComm exercise — amateur radio operators set up portable stations without mains power and make as many QSOs as possible in 24 or 48 hours. The goal is to test: - Portable equipment - Backup power - Operating procedures - Team coordination

In the USA this is organised by ARRL every June. In Europe and Croatia, clubs organise their own Field Day activities — follow the HRS calendar and club websites.

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• Emergency Procedures — radio discipline and message protocols
  • Winlink — digital messages without internet
  • Portable Operations — portable station
  • Safety — safe setup in crisis conditions
  • Associations and Organisations — HRS contact and coordination

docs.title: Winlink — Email Without the Internet
docs.text:
Winlink is a global network that enables sending and receiving email messages via radio, without relying on internet infrastructure. In crisis situations — when telephone networks and the internet are unavailable — Winlink remains operational as long as there is a radio signal.

Winlink is used by emergency services, the Red Cross, Coast Guard, ARES teams, and thousands of amateur radio operators for regular digital communication and EmComm exercises.

docs.title: How Winlink Works
docs.text:
The Winlink system has three elements:

[Your computer + radio]
        │
        │ (RF signal — HF or VHF/UHF)
        ▼
[Gateway / RMS station]
(amateur radio station connected to the internet)
        │
        │ (internet)
        ▼
[CMS — Winlink Central Message Server]
(cloud system that stores and forwards messages)
        │
        ├── → Winlink users (retrieve via radio or web)
        └── → Regular internet email recipients

Key advantage: Your message travels by radio to a gateway station that has internet access. You have no internet — but the gateway does, and it forwards the message onwards. Via HF (shortwave) you can connect to a gateway in another country thousands of kilometres away — even if all local infrastructure is completely down.

docs.title: Winlink Operating Modes
docs.text:
Winlink supports several data transmission modes:

VARA HFVARA FMPactorARDOPPacket (AX.25)

The most modern HF mode, high throughput, robust in difficult conditions. - Frequency range: HF (3–30 MHz) - Software: VARA HF modem (free for 1,200 bps, paid for full speed) - Recommended speed: up to 8,400 bps in good conditions - Recommended for EmComm HF work

FM version for VHF/UHF repeaters and simplex frequencies. - Frequency range: VHF/UHF (144 MHz, 432 MHz) - Fast and reliable for local connections - Excellent for coordination within a town or region - Recommended for local EmComm VHF work

Older but robust HF mode, popular in maritime and ARES communities. - Requires a hardware Pactor TNC modem (SCS, ~€400–600) - Excellent throughput for HF, especially on DX links - Used in maritime and expedition stations

Free open-source HF protocol, somewhat slower than VARA but with no licensing cost. - Good option for beginners without the VARA licence cost - Built into Winlink Express

Classic VHF packet radio, reliable for local networks. - In use since the 1980s, well proven - 1,200 bps (or 9,600 bps on UHF) - Foundation for the APRS infrastructure

docs.title: Software — Winlink Express
docs.text:
Winlink Express is the free client software for Windows that integrates all Winlink functionality:

  • Download: winlink.org/downloads
  • Free for amateur radio operators (register with your callsign)
  • Supports all Winlink modes (VARA, ARDOP, Packet, Telnet)
  • Built-in message editor similar to an email client
  • ICS forms — standardised civil protection forms for EmComm

Winlink Express on Linux/Mac

Winlink via the Pat client (free, open-source) is available on macOS and Linux: getpat.io. Alternatively, Winlink Express runs under Wine on Linux.

docs.title: Minimum configuration (VHF/UHF)
docs.text: Component Example Price Computer / laptop Older Windows laptop is fine — VHF/UHF transceiver Icom IC-2300H, Yaesu FT-8900R €100–200 SignaLink USB Audio interface radio-PC ~€90–110 Radio cable Specific to transceiver model €10–30

docs.title: Advanced configuration (HF with VARA)
docs.text:
Component Example Price HF transceiver Icom IC-7300, Yaesu FT-991A €700–1,500 VARA HF licence For full speed (optional) ~$69 CAT cable USB-Serial for CAT control €10–30 SignaLink USB Audio interface ~€90–110

Icom IC-7300 and built-in sound card

The Icom IC-7300 has a built-in USB audio interface and CAT — you connect a single USB cable to the computer and Winlink Express recognises the radio without a SignaLink adapter. An ideal combination for Winlink.

docs.title: ICS Forms in Winlink
docs.text:
ICS (Incident Command System) forms are standardised forms used by emergency services worldwide to document and transmit critical information. Winlink Express embeds ICS forms in digital format:

Form Purpose ICS-213 General Message ICS-214 Activity Log ICS-309 Communications Log Red Cross DDS American Red Cross disaster damage report HICS-215A Hospital communications form

In an EmComm situation, a hospital, shelter, or coordination centre can receive an ICS-213 form sent wirelessly via Winlink and process it as a structured document — without re-entering the same information manually.

docs.title: Winlink Gateway Network in the Croatian Region
docs.text:
Regional HF and VHF Winlink gateways accessible from Croatia:

Station Country Mode Frequency 9A5ST-10 Croatia Pactor/VARA HF (verify at winlink.org) OE stations Austria VARA HF Multiple HF frequencies I stations Italy VARA HF Multiple HF frequencies S5 stations Slovenia VARA FM / HF VHF + HF

For the current gateway list and frequencies, use the map at winlink.org/map.

docs.title: Practical Test — Sending a Test Message
docs.text:
Before you need Winlink in a real EmComm situation, practise by sending test messages:

  1. Install Winlink Express and register (your amateur callsign is the login)
  2. Configure your SignaLink or CAT connection to the radio
  3. Select a mode (for starters: Telnet if you have internet — to test without radio)
  4. Send a message to WLNK-1@winlink.org — a bot that responds with a confirmation
  5. Check your inbox for the reply

Once the test mode works, repeat the same process without internet (VARA FM or HF) — that is the real test.

Monthly Winlink exercise

Every first Sunday of the month, a global Winlink exercise encourages operators to send a test EmComm message. Details at winlink.org. By participating you practise the procedure and your station remains "visible" in the system.

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• RMZO — Croatian EmComm organisation and frequencies
  • Emergency Procedures — radio discipline and messages
  • Portable Operations — portable station for EmComm
  • Operating Modes — digital modes in general

docs.title: Operating
docs.text: Here you will learn how to actually make contacts, which frequencies to use, and what rules of conduct apply on the air.

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• Band Plans: Frequency allocation by bands.
  • Modes: Detailed overview of analog and digital modes.
  • QSO Procedures: How to start and end a contact, Q-Codes.
  • Logbooks and QSL Cards: Confirming contacts.

docs.title: Band Plans
docs.text:
A band plan is an agreement on the use of parts of amateur bands for different modes of operation. Respecting the band plan is a matter of amateur radio ethics and culture.

Here we present the plan for IARU Region 1 (Europe), with notes for Region 2 (North & South America) where significant differences exist.

docs.title: 160m (1.8 MHz)
docs.text:
"Top Band". Night band.

Frequency (kHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 1810 - 1838 CW USA: 1800 - 2000 kHz 1838 - 1840 Digital (FT8 @ 1840) 1840 - 2000 SSB / CW

docs.title: 80m (3.5 MHz)
docs.text:
Most popular band for local contacts.

Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 3.500 - 3.570 CW 3.570 - 3.600 Digital (FT8 @ 3.573) 3.600 - 3.800 SSB (LSB) USA goes up to 4.000 MHz! (Phone: 3.600 - 4.000)

docs.title: 40m (7 MHz)
docs.text:
Reliable band for Europe and DX.

Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 7.000 - 7.040 CW 7.040 - 7.060 Digital (FT8 @ 7.074) 7.060 - 7.200 SSB (LSB) USA goes up to 7.300 MHz! (Phone starts at 7.125)

docs.title: 20m (14 MHz)
docs.text:
"King of DX".

Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 14.000 - 14.070 CW 14.070 - 14.100 Digital (FT8 @ 14.074) 14.100 - 14.350 SSB (USB) IARU R2 is the same.

docs.title: 2m (144 MHz)
docs.text:
Most popular VHF band.

Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 144.000 - 144.150 CW / SSB (DX) 144.150 - 144.400 SSB / MGM 144.800 APRS USA: APRS is on 144.390 145.000 - 145.175 Repeaters (Input) USA band goes to 148 MHz! 145.200 - 145.575 FM Simplex 145.500 FM Calling Channel USA: 146.520 145.600 - 145.775 Repeaters (Output) Shift is the same (-600 kHz)

docs.title: 70cm (432 MHz)
docs.text: Frequency (MHz) Usage (EU) Note (USA) 432.000 - 432.400 CW / SSB USA band: 420 - 450 MHz 433.000 - 434.000 FM Simplex / Repeaters 446.000 - 446.200 PMR446 (License-free) USA: FRS/GMRS (462/467 MHz)

docs.title: Logbooks and QSL Cards
docs.text: Every contact made should be logged. This is a legal requirement and part of the tradition.

docs.title: Logbook
docs.text: The logbook must contain: - Date and time (UTC). - Correspondent's call sign. - Frequency (or band). - Mode. - Report (RST).

docs.title: Paper vs. Electronic
docs.text: Today, most use computer programs (loggers) such as N1MM, DXKeeper, or Ham Radio Deluxe. They automatically record time and frequency (if connected to the radio).

docs.title: QSL Cards
docs.text: A QSL card is a written confirmation of a contact. It is the "final courtesy" of a ham. - Paper QSL: Sent directly by mail or (cheaper) via the national association's QSL bureau. - Electronic QSL: - LoTW (Logbook of The World): ARRL system. Officially recognized for awards (DXCC). - eQSL: Popular, but less "official". - ClubLog: Excellent for analysis and OQRS (Online QSL Request System).

docs.title: Modes
docs.text: Radio amateurs use different types of modulation to transmit information. Each mode has its pros, cons, and specific "sound".

docs.title: CW (Continuous Wave / Telegraphy)
docs.text: The oldest form of communication. Uses Morse code (dots and dashes). * Pros: Range (gets through when nothing else can), simple equipment, narrow bandwidth. * Sound: Rhythmic beeping. * Listen to CW (Video)

docs.title: SSB (Single Sideband)
docs.text: The most popular mode for voice on Shortwave (HF). An advanced version of AM that uses less energy. * LSB (Lower Sideband): Used below 10 MHz (40m, 80m). * USB (Upper Sideband): Used above 10 MHz (20m, 17m...) and on VHF/UHF. * Sound: Human voice, but sounds like "Donald Duck" if you are not exactly on frequency. * Listen to SSB (Video)

docs.title: FM (Frequency Modulation)
docs.text: Standard for local contacts on VHF/UHF (handhelds). * Pros: Clear sound, no noise (if signal is strong). * Cons: Requires wide bandwidth, shorter range compared to SSB/CW.

docs.title: Digital Modes (FT8, RTTY...)
docs.text: Computer and radio working together. * FT8: Currently the most popular mode. Allows contacts with very low power and inaudible signals. The contact is automated. * RTTY (Teletype): Older digital mode, sounds like an "80s video game". * FT8 Sound: Sounds like multiple tones warbling. * Listen to FT8 (Video)

docs.title: Waterfall Display
docs.text: Modern radios and software display signals visually. * X Axis: Frequency. * Y Axis: Time. * Color: Signal strength. * Allows you to "see" stations before you hear them.

docs.title: QSO Procedures
docs.text: Making a contact has its rules to ensure efficient and understandable communication.

docs.title: Basic Terms
docs.text:
• CQ: General call ("Calling Any Station").
  • DE: From (used in CW or digital modes).
  • K: Over.
  • 73: Best Regards.

docs.title: Example Contact (SSB)
docs.text:
1. Call: "CQ CQ CQ, this is 9A1AA, 9A1AA calling and listening."
  2. Reply: "9A1AA, this is DL1BBB."
  3. Report: "DL1BBB, good day. Your signal is 59 (five-nine), name is Ivan, locator JN75. 9A1AA."
  4. Confirmation: "9A1AA, thanks Ivan. Your signal is also 59, my name is Hans. 73!"
  5. End: "Thanks Hans, 73 and hope to hear you again. DL1BBB de 9A1AA."

docs.title: RST System
docs.text:
The report consists of 3 digits (for CW) or 2 (for SSB): - R (Readability): 1-5. - S (Signal Strength): 1-9. - T (Tone): 1-9 (CW only).

Example: 599 (Perfect signal on CW).

docs.title: Q-Codes
docs.text: Q-codes are standardized three-letter codes starting with the letter Q. Originally developed for telegraphy to speed up communication, they are now also used in voice modes.

docs.title: Frequently Used Q-Codes
docs.text: Code Question Answer / Advice QRA What is the name of your station? The name of my station is... QRG What is my exact frequency? Your exact frequency is ... kHz. QRL Are you busy? I am busy (please do not interfere). QRM Is my transmission being interfered with? You are being interfered with (1-5). QRN Are you troubled by static? I am troubled by static (1-5). QRO Shall I increase power? Increase power. QRP Shall I decrease power? Decrease power. QRT Shall I stop sending? Stop sending. QRZ Who is calling me? You are being called by... (on ... kHz). QSB Are my signals fading? Your signals are fading. QSL Can you acknowledge receipt? I am acknowledging receipt. QSO Can you communicate with...? I can communicate with... QSY Shall I change to transmission on another frequency? Change to transmission on ... kHz. QTH What is your position in latitude and longitude (or by any other indication)? My position is...

docs.title: All Q-Codes (Amateur Radio)
docs.text: Code Meaning QRA Name of station / Address QRB Distance between stations QRC Who pays the charges (commercial, rare in ham) QRD Where are you bound and where are you from? QRE Estimated time of arrival? QRF Are you returning? QRG Exact frequency QRH Does my frequency vary? QRI Tone of transmission (T in RST) QRJ Are you receiving me badly? QRK Readability of signals (R in RST) QRL Frequency is busy QRM Interference from other stations QRN Atmospheric interference (static) QRO High power QRP Low power QRQ Send faster QRR Are you ready for automatic operation? QRRS Land SOS (rarely used) QRS Send slower QRT Stop sending / Shutting down QRU Have you anything for me? QRV I am ready QRW Shall I inform...? QRX Wait a moment QRY What is my turn? QRZ Who is calling me? QSA Signal strength (S in RST) QSB Fading QSD Keying is defective QSK Can you hear me between your signals (Break-in)? QSL Acknowledge receipt (card) QSM Shall I repeat the last message? QSN Did you hear me? QSO Contact QSP Will you relay to...? QSQ Have you a doctor? QSR Shall I repeat the call? QSS What working frequency will you use? QST General call to all amateurs (message) QSU Shall I send or reply on this frequency? QSV Shall I send a series of V's? QSW Will you send on this frequency? QSX Will you listen to...? QSY Change frequency QSZ Shall I send each word twice? QTA Shall I cancel message? QTB Do you agree with my counting of words? QTC How many messages have you to send? QTH Location (City, Locator) QTR Exact time

docs.title: Regulations & Licensing
docs.text: Here you can find all information related to legal regulations and the process of obtaining an amateur radio license.

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• How to become a Ham?: Information on exams for P and A classes.
  • Croatian Regulations: Laws and regulations by HAKOM and HRS.
  • International Regulations: CEPT and IARU recommendations.
  • Call Signs: Structure and allocation of call signs in Croatia.

docs.title: Useful Documents
docs.text:
• Ordinance on Amateur Radio Communications
  • Exam questions (HRS)

docs.title: Call Signs
docs.text: A call sign is a unique identifier for an amateur radio station.

docs.title: Structure (Croatia)
docs.text:
Croatian call signs consist of three parts:

  1. Prefix: 9A (indicates Croatia).
  2. Number: 1 - 9 (historically indicated region, today mostly arbitrary but often follows: 1-Zagreb, 2-Dalmatia, 3-Istria/Littoral, 4-Slavonia...). 0 is used for special signs.
  3. Suffix: 1, 2, or 3 letters.
    • 1 letter: Clubs with long tradition or special signs (e.g., 9A1A).
    • 2 letters: Standard personal signs (e.g., 9A2AA) or clubs.
    • 3 letters: Standard personal signs (e.g., 9A3AAA).

docs.title: Types of Call Signs
docs.text:
• Personal Call Signs: Assigned to individuals after passing the exam.
  • Club Call Signs: Assigned to radio clubs.
  • Special Call Signs: For contests, anniversaries, or expeditions (e.g., 9A80Z, 9A0HQ).

docs.title: Identification
docs.text: During operation, a ham must regularly transmit their call sign (at the start, end, and every 10-15 minutes during contact). When operating from another country (CEPT), place that country's prefix before your call sign (e.g., S5/9A1AA if in Slovenia). When operating portable/mobile, add the suffix /P (portable), /M (mobile), or /MM (maritime mobile).

docs.title: Croatian Regulations (Hrvatski propisi)
docs.text: Radio amateurism in Croatia is regulated by laws under the jurisdiction of state institutions to prevent abuse of the RF spectrum and ensure coordinated technical frameworks.

docs.title: 1. Electronic Communications Act
docs.text: This is the fundamental national law regulating the use of the electronic network and RF spectrum in Croatia for civil and military services. The main aspects for an amateur focus on the confiscation protocol of illegal gear and strict exposure guidelines (MPE/ICNIRP standards) related to constructed antennas.

docs.title: 2. Ordinance on Amateur Radio Communications
docs.text: Consider this the crucial "bible of regulations". It dictates exactly what decibel power outputs and prefix bounds you adhere to as an active station. - Establishes operators' classes and testing formats. - Dictates boundaries of your permitted wave spectrum usage limits.

docs.title: Call Sign Rules
docs.text: In Croatia, all universal primary international identifiers start with the 9A prefix. Changing location temporarily holds obligations. You are required to amend suffixes during those operations: * /P (Portable): For hiking, battery, or SOTA operations from temporary stations. * /M (Mobile): While in motion on a road structure driving a car. * /MM (Maritime Mobile): Operating communications from oceanic vessels/boats. * /AM (Aeronautical Mobile): Transmitting RF if flying on a plane.

docs.title: HAKOM (Croatian Regulatory Authority for Network Industries)
docs.text: The primary inspecting arm. HAKOM sets the licensing testing, oversees the technical exams, and regulates limits. If you radiate too strongly creating high EMF interference issues in neighborhoods, their terrain vehicles hold the legal ability to confiscate your rig.

docs.title: HRS (Croatian Amateur Radio Association)
docs.text: Your united hub - organizing global negotiations for the hobby. In terms of benefits natively, HRS acts worldwide saving members immense costs by handling all QSL cards out/in natively through the free "QSL bureau".

docs.title: Legislative History and Timeline
docs.text:
Year Milestone for Croatian Ham Operations 1992 Implementation of the initial independent laws transitioning into the globally standardized 9A prefix. 2003 Morse Code dropping globally removed limits for accessing the full HF band without prior CW knowledge. 2012 Implementations with CEPT T/R agreements bringing European synchronizations to low frequencies. 2021 Upgrading the exposure rules with heavier EMF requirements due to power density proximity laws.

Agencies strictly forbid ignoring interference.

Respecting the Electronic Communications Act is critical. Ignoring continuous harmful interference calls from your station is a definite way to permanently lose the license and have the equipment confiscated by HAKOM's legal jurisdiction!

docs.title: Amateur Radio Ethics and Operating Guidelines
docs.text: The hobbyist and experimental radio spectrum is a precious natural resource. Amateur radio doesn't survive merely by the sheer technical force of laws forcing you to respect frequency; it survives by a "Gentleman's rule".

docs.title: The Amateur's Code (Paul M. Segal, W9EEA, 1928.)
docs.text:
The history and standard traces back to the inception of mass spectrum utilization framed through a legendary "Amateur's Code" displaying essential gold standards authored in 1928:

The Amateur is Considerate: He never knowingly uses the air in such a way as to lessen the pleasure of others. The Amateur is Loyal: He offers his loyalty, encouragement and support to his fellow radio amateurs, his local club, and the League. The Amateur is Progressive: He keeps his station abreast of science. It is built well and efficiently. The Amateur is Friendly: Slow and patient sending when requested, friendly advice and counsel to the beginner, kindly assistance, cooperation, and consideration. The Amateur is Balanced: Radio is his hobby. He never allows it to interfere with any of the duties he owes to his home, his job, his school, or his community. The Amateur is Patriotic: His knowledge and his station are always ready for the service of his country and his community.

docs.title: 10 Proper Behavioral Rules Over Operating
docs.text: Remember the etiquette sequence upon powering up: 1. Listen first! Never dump transmissions into a void without carefully scanning the background noise. 2. Verify "QRL?"! Transmit asking "Is the frequency in use please?". You might save someone's exhausting hour-long QSO efforts. 3. Keep transmissions short. Avoid hour-long monologues. Let the partner input sentences in their loop. 4. Correct nicely. Don't aggressively yell at strangers regarding flawed phonetics. Calmly demonstrate proper NATO spelling instead. 5. Signal your ends. Explicitly utilize phrases like Over, Clear, or CW prosigns (K or SK). 6. Only use verified callsigns! Do NOT yell "Hey Tony pick it up!" Make sure to provide your callsign identifier regularly (every ten minutes rules depend heavily per country). 7. Drop power. Use the lowest Watt output sufficient to cover the needed space. 8. Wait out pile-ups. Don't bust in screaming your call if an expedition points fingers requesting specific tails. 9. Avoid ugly topics. Hardcore religion, deep volatile politics, or hostilities must be abandoned entirely out of gentlemanly respect for the hobby. 10. Welcome equality. Treat operators equally regardless of gender/position and embrace "Old Men" OM conventions.

docs.title: Sanctioned Behaviors (NEVER DO)
docs.text:
Broadcasting or Sales Pitches!

The station is NEITHER an FM advert radio, nor an internal selling tactic to order taxis. Broadcasting musical beats or using stations for business exchanges carries hundreds of euros in fines with outright irreversible suspensions!

docs.title: QRM/QRN Trolls (Intentional interference responses)
docs.text: What if you hit a void while calling resulting in intentional interference, people playing digital buzz over you, or insulting the communication? 1. Stay utterly Silent. The moment you press key saying "Who is interfering?", the aggressor succeeded and will double down. Slide 5 kHz off the channel, ignoring the troll causes his expensive power amplifiers to basically burn completely useless. 2. File Reports. If you notice structural malicious usage, logging times alongside the IARU/HAKOM reports drives their inspection vehicles directly to finding the illegally radiating station!

docs.title: Priorities: Distress and MAYDAY Communications!
docs.text:
If within your chatter or scanning you hear specific urgent phrases utilized 3 times: MAYDAY, MAYDAY MAYDAY, EMERGENCY EMERGENCY or an SOS CW pattern ... --- ...:

Stop and Evacuate the Spectrum Immediately!

Interrupting your chatter instantly is an absolute legally mandated action. Your sole duty becomes observing the distress communication. Attempt mapping their location completely. NEVER transmit anything hindering their rescue and only help redirecting emergency networks if explicitly requested to do so!

docs.title: Mentoring: Being an "Elmer"
docs.text: The classical "Elmer" term marks heavy gratitude targeting senior operatives taking terrified newcomers safely under their supportive wings ensuring the amateur craft survives. Teach them how to connect properly locally and respect laws without forcing expensive purchases upon them.

docs.title: How to become a Radio Amateur?
docs.text: The path to an amateur radio license is achieved through a professional exam before regulatory bodies or authorized associations or local clubs. The license guarantees that you respect the foundations of communications and don't mistakenly operate on military or emergency service reserved bounds.

docs.title: Amateur Radio License Classes
docs.text: Class Full Name Frequencies (Privileges) Max Power Usage N (Novice Nat) National Initial License Narrow VHF/UHF only. up to 10W Handheld local FM usage. B (Beginner / P) CEPT Novice License All bands within specified bounds. up to 100W Excellent start for HF work, ideal for most hobbyists. A (Advanced) CEPT HAREC Absolute access to all amateur bands strictly by bandplan up to 1500W Full freedom to build amplifiers and international traveling portability.

docs.title: Who can take the exam (Prerequisites)
docs.text: The test is open to virtually anyone; age boundaries go as low as 12 in many countries emphasizing youth integrations in STEM subjects. No engineering college records are required, merely the will to learn radio waves.

docs.title: How and Where to Apply
docs.text:
• Preparation: Find your closest local radio-club. They organize group study courses running bi-annually.
  • Books: Access manuals locally or via web platforms connected to the National IARU associated body.
  • Exam day: Takes place locally or at the national telecommunication headquarters as written Multiple-choice exams.

docs.title: What is the exam actually like?
docs.text:
• Total Questions: Varies between 30 (Novice/Beginner) to 45 (HAREC/A-class).
  • Time: 45 to 60 minutes.
  • Passing Grade: A substantial 60-70% accuracy is necessary. Regulatory law topics usually mandate 100% correct answering due to safety reasons.
  • Format: Strictly written Multiple-Choice. Morse code is NO LONGER mandatory in Europe.

docs.title: Core Exam Topics
docs.text:
1. Technique and Electronics – Circuits, ohm's law, filters.
  2. Operations on the band – Q-codes, procedure, conventions.
  3. Safety and EMF – High voltage danger, exposure limits, grounding.
  4. Relevant Regional Regulations (Laws) – Allowances, agency limits.

docs.title: International Comparison
docs.text:
USAUKGermany (DE)
  • Technician: Local ops, mostly VHF/UHF FM repeaters.
  • General: Main HF capability and DX access. CEPT Novice-ish.
  • Amateur Extra: Full privileges, elite frequencies, HAREC equivalent.
  • Foundation: Getting started. 10W limits.
  • Intermediate: 50W, constructing and altering equipment.
  • Full Licence: Unrestricted power outputs and full HAREC privileges.
  • Klasse E: Entry German class, equivalent to Novice across EU.
  • Klasse A: HAREC equivalent with full Kilowatt outputs.

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
docs.text:
1. Is Morse code knowledge mandatory? No. The CW code test was phased out in the early 2000s globally.

2. Can I pass the electronics bit without a degree? Absolutely. The level requested relies on simple Physics logic and applying Ohm's Law (\(R = U / I\)).

3. Do I start at the bottom or can I jump to Class A directly? If you have the knowledge and your regional agency permits, yes, you can skip to Class A.

Do not transmit without the clearance papers!

Using PTT (hitting the mic) without receiving the official operator license and station ID registration holds very high fiscal penalties. Agencies use triangulated sweeps via radio direction finders to sniff illegally broadcasted activity.

docs.title: International Regulations
docs.text: Amateur radio is a global hobby, so international coordination is crucial.

docs.title: ITU (International Telecommunication Union)
docs.text: The UN agency that coordinates the use of the radio frequency spectrum globally. - Radio Regulations (RR): International regulations defining the amateur service.

docs.title: IARU (International Amateur Radio Union)
docs.text: Represents the interests of radio amateurs before the ITU. - Divided into 3 regions: 1. Region 1: Europe, Africa, Middle East, Northern Asia. 2. Region 2: The Americas. 3. Region 3: Rest of Asia, Australia, Oceania.

docs.title: CEPT (European Conference of Postal and Telecommunications Administrations)
docs.text: European organization that harmonizes regulations. - Recommendation T/R 61-01: Allows hams with a CEPT license (Class A in Croatia) to operate in other signatory countries without additional bureaucracy (up to 3 months). - Recommendation T/R 61-02: Harmonized Amateur Radio Examination Certificate (HAREC).

docs.title: What does this mean for you?
docs.text: If you pass Class A in Croatia, your license is recognized in most European countries (and many others). You can travel and operate your station using the prefix of the country you are visiting (e.g., DL/9A1AA).

docs.title: License Classes
docs.text: Amateur radio licenses are split into a tier-based progression system. From beginner classes intended for local FM communications up to advanced expert tiers with full privileges and power limits on long wave HF communications.

docs.title: N Class (Novice – National Tier)
docs.text: The N class is an extremely basic approach for younger members or complete trainees meant to operate almost exclusively upon entering club activities using handhelds. - Conditions: Basic knowledge above local environments. High focus merely on simple operating safety. - Frequencies: Purely short VHF/UHF bands (e.g. 144 MHz with repeaters). - Modes: Primarily Voice Analogs via FM using small unboosted output powers. Cannot utilize global reaching HF.

docs.title: B Class ("Novice" CEPT Tier)
docs.text: In most central European nations, this class is the primary entry for adults. Synced with the Europe-wide "CEPT Novice", it relies on a serious test bringing basic mathematical electronics formulas. - Power & Bands: Usually greenlights operations up to 100 Watts! Allowances include specially narrowed slices of shortwaves (80, 40, 20m) for international DX chasing.

docs.title: A Class (CEPT HAREC – Advanced Tier)
docs.text: Holding an A-Class signifies an advanced engineering engineering understanding. You hold complete legal CEPT authorizations based on "T/R 61-01". - Privilege & Output: HAREC designation denotes your ability to completely construct and revamp components. You have unrestrained access to the whole ITU HF spectrum and can utilize powers scaling into the massive gigawatts (usually limited regionally from 1000 - 1500 Watts).

docs.title: Progressing as an Operator
docs.text: After initiating the journey in Class B, you do not have to wait years to step up; subsequent tests allow you to skip straight to the HAREC tier if you possess the knowledge needed! Do note there are absolutely zero repercussions. Trying to shoot for the top Tier A test and missing the threshold, mostly gracefully slips you seamlessly down granting you the Novice Tier B anyway as a secondary pass reward!

docs.title: Worldwide Equivalencies
docs.text:
What do you call an A-Class in America?

USA - FCC ClassesUnited Kingdom (Ofcom)Germany
  • Technician: N-class locally oriented on VHF.
  • General: Novice Tier CEPT counterpart. Massive global HF reach.
  • Amateur Extra: HAREC Expert Tier. Access to rare bandwidths.
  • Foundation Licence: Minimal practical entry around 10W limits.
  • Intermediate: Medium 50W output building phase.
  • Full Licence: HAREC designation with kilowatt-capable clearance.
  • Klasse E: CEPT Novice Tier with medium power.
  • Klasse A: HAREC Tier holding maximum station capabilities.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text: Useful links, tools, and software for radio amateurs.

docs.title: Online Tools
docs.text:
• Dictionary (Abbreviations): Explanations of terms and abbreviations.
  • PSK Reporter: Real-time digital mode activity map.
  • DX Summit: DX Cluster.
  • Reverse Beacon Network (RBN): Automatic CW/RTTY signal monitoring.

docs.title: Croatia (HR) - Featured
docs.text:
• Croatian Amateur Radio Association (HRS): National organization.
  • HAKOM: Regulatory authority.

docs.title: IARU Region 1 (Europe, Africa, Middle East)
docs.text:
• IARU Region 1: Official Region 1 site.
  • DARC: German association (excellent technical resources).
  • RSGB: British association.

docs.title: IARU Region 2 (The Americas)
docs.text:
• ARRL: American association (largest knowledge base).
  • RAC: Canadian association.

docs.title: IARU Region 3 (Asia, Pacific)
docs.text:
• JARL: Japanese association.
  • WIA: Australian association.

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
• WSJT-X: Standard for FT8/FT4.
  • N1MM Logger+: Most popular contest log.
  • Ham Radio Deluxe: All-in-one solution.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Preparing for your amateur radio license exam requires more than just memorizing answers; it demands a solid understanding of the concepts so you can safely and effectively operate a radio station.

docs.title: Study Plan (2 to 3 Month Recommendation)
docs.text:
Daily Priority — Consistency Rule

Studying the material shouldn't be crammed. Doing 30-45 minutes of active learning EVERY DAY strengthens your knowledge significantly better than 4-hour weekend cram sessions.

Week Focus Area and Topic Manual References Verification Goal Weeks 1 & 2 Regulations, licenses Regulations Can identify limits, licensing classes, and offenses. Weeks 3 & 4 Basic Electronics Electronics Run through Ohm's law and LC frequency calculations. Weeks 5 & 6 Antennas and Propagation Antennas & Propagation Know differences between dipole, SWR, and ionospheric reflection. Weeks 7 & 8 Modes, Operating Procedure Modes, and Operating Know which frequencies use LSB vs USB, and standard Q codes. Weeks 9 & 10 Exposure limit, Safety, EMF Safety & Grounding Recognize life-threatening hazards and installation safety limits. Weeks 11 & 12 Repetition and Mock Exams Entire curriculum Pass the mock exams with over 80% every time.

docs.title: Regulations and Licenses
docs.text: Know your legal limits. Review License Classes and National Regulations. KEY TIP: The Golden Rule of Class A. To operate internationally with portability rights across CEPT nations, you must hold the Class A (HAREC) equivalent.

docs.title: Basic Electronics
docs.text: Pure radio physics. Review Electronics. KEY TIP: Memorize three formulas: Ohm's Law (\(U=R·I\)), Power computation (\(P=U·I\)), and LC Resonant Frequency (\(f=1/2\pi\sqrt{LC}\)).

docs.title: Example Exam Questions (With explanations)
docs.text:
Q1: Which license class allows the use of all amateur frequency bands? Answer: Class A (CEPT Class).

Why?: Lower classes restrict bands and bounds, whereas Class A provides global T/R 61-01 CEPT privileges.

Q2: Resistors R1 = 100Ω and R2 = 150Ω are connected in series. Total resistance? Answer: 250 Ω.

Why?: In a series circuit, current goes through components consecutively. Their resistance adds up.

Q3: Which component defines the resonant frequency in an LC circuit? Answer: BOTH the inductor (L) and the capacitor (C).

Why?: The math equation for resonant frequency relies on the product of L*C.

Q4: Which Ionospheric layer is primary for global HF DX daytime propagation? Answer: The "F2" layer at roughly 400 km.

Why?: Lower D and E layers fade or absorb signals. F2 remains night and day for skip propagation.

Q5: What does an SWR of 3:1 imply? Answer: A mismatch returning up to 50% power back to the transmitter.

Why?: Anything above 2:1 is bad and turns output energy into heat in your finals.

Q6: What sideband is used below 10 MHz? Answer: LSB (Lower Side Band).

Why?: By international convention, below 10MHz LSB is used, above it USB is used.

Q7: Why do power supply capacitors remain dangerous after pulling the plug? Answer: They hold deadly high-voltage charges for up to 30 minutes.

Why?: The discharging bleeder resistors take time, and touching them bridges the gap and unloads that voltage into your body.

docs.title: Exam Day Tips
docs.text:
• Bring essentials: ID, pens, allowable basic (non-programmable) calculators.
  • Tactic: Read EVERY question to its absolute final comma. Eliminate obviously wrong options first.
  • Blocked/Panicking? Leave the question blank, continue rolling through, and return to it later. Let the anxiety subside instead of rushing a guess.

docs.title: Online Resources
docs.text:
• Ham Test Online (hamstudy.org): An amazing tool focusing heavily on the FCC syllabus but conceptually identical to physics in CEPT.
  • RadioAmateur.Eu: Good for generating CEPT-oriented European quizzes.

docs.title: Ham Radio Dictionary (Abbreviations)
docs.text:
Here you can find explanations of the most common abbreviations used by radio amateurs.

A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | I | L | M | O | P | Q | R | S | T | U | V | W | X | Y | Z

docs.title: A
docs.text:
• AF (Audio Frequency): Audio frequency. The frequency range audible to the human ear (approx. 20 Hz to 20 kHz).
  • AGC (Automatic Gain Control): Automatic gain control. A circuit that maintains a constant output level despite variations in input signal strength.
  • ALC (Automatic Level Control): A circuit in a transmitter that prevents overmodulation or overdriving the power amplifier.
  • AM (Amplitude Modulation): Amplitude modulation. A modulation technique where information is encoded by varying the amplitude of the carrier wave.
  • APRS (Automatic Packet Reporting System): APRS. A system for real-time digital communications of information of immediate value (position, messages, telemetry).

docs.title: B
docs.text:
• BCI (Broadcast Interference): Interference caused by broadcast stations (e.g., FM radio or TV).
  • BFO (Beat Frequency Oscillator): Beat frequency oscillator. An oscillator in a receiver used to create an audible tone for CW and SSB reception.
  • BK (Break-in): QSK. The ability to hear the other station between your own Morse code characters (Full Break-in).

docs.title: C
docs.text:
• CEPT: CEPT. European body that regulates the mutual recognition of amateur radio licenses.
  • CQ: CQ (call). A general call to all stations ("Calling any station").
  • CW (Continuous Wave): Continuous wave. A method of communication by keying a carrier on and off (Morse code).

docs.title: D
docs.text:
• dB (Decibel): Decibel. A logarithmic unit used to express the ratio of two values (e.g., power or voltage).
  • Dipole: Dipole antenna. The simplest type of antenna, consisting of two conductive elements, typically half a wavelength long in total.
  • Dummy Load: Dummy load. A resistor (usually 50 ohms) connected to a transmitter instead of an antenna for testing purposes without radiating.
  • DX (Distance): DXing. Abbreviation for distance or contact with a distant/rare station.

docs.title: E
docs.text:
• EME (Earth-Moon-Earth): EME communication. A technique relying on the reflection of radio signals from the Moon.
  • ERP (Effective Radiated Power): Effective radiated power. The product of the power supplied to the antenna and its gain relative to a half-wave dipole.

docs.title: F
docs.text:
• FM (Frequency Modulation): Frequency modulation. A modulation technique where information is encoded by varying the frequency of the carrier wave.
  • FT8: FT8. A popular digital mode designed for weak-signal communication.

docs.title: G
docs.text:
• GND (Ground): Ground (electricity). Electrical connection to the earth for safety and reference potential.
  • GP (Ground Plane): Monopole antenna. A vertical antenna (monopole) mounted over a ground plane (often radials) to simulate a perfect conductive surface.

docs.title: H
docs.text:
• HF (High Frequency): High frequency. The radio frequency range from 3 to 30 MHz (Shortwave).
  • HT (Handheld Transceiver): Walkie-talkie. A portable radio with battery and antenna in a single unit.

docs.title: I
docs.text:
• IARU: International Amateur Radio Union. An alliance of national amateur radio societies.
  • ITU: International Telecommunication Union. The UN agency for information and communication technologies.

docs.title: L
docs.text:
• LDS (Long Delayed Echo): Long delayed echo. A phenomenon where a radio signal returns to the sender after a significant delay.
  • LSB (Lower Sideband): Single-sideband modulation. The lower sideband of an AM signal.

docs.title: M
docs.text: • MUF (Maximum Usable Frequency): Maximum usable frequency. The highest frequency that can be used for communication between two points via ionospheric reflection.

docs.title: O
docs.text: • OM (Old Man): Slang for a male amateur radio operator, regardless of age. A friendly term.

docs.title: P
docs.text:
• PA (Power Amplifier): RF power amplifier. A device that increases the power of a radio signal before transmission.
  • PEP (Peak Envelope Power): Peak envelope power. The standard power measurement for SSB transmitters.
  • PTT (Push To Talk): Push-to-talk. A switch that switches the device from receive to transmit mode.

docs.title: Q
docs.text: (See Q-Codes for a detailed list) - Q-code: Q code. A standardized collection of three-letter codes.

docs.title: R
docs.text:
• RIT (Receiver Incremental Tuning): A control that allows fine-tuning of the receive frequency without affecting the transmit frequency.
  • RST: R-S-T system. A system for reporting signal quality (Readability, Strength, Tone).
  • RTTY (Radioteletype): Radioteletype. A telecommunications system for transmitting text via radio.

docs.title: S
docs.text:
• SWR (Standing Wave Ratio): Standing wave ratio. A measure of impedance matching between the antenna and transmitter.
  • SSB (Single Sideband): Single-sideband modulation. An efficient voice transmission method using only one sideband.
  • Squelch: Squelch. A circuit that suppresses the audio output of a receiver in the absence of a strong input signal.

docs.title: T
docs.text:
• TVI (Television Interference): Interference caused by a radio transmitter to television reception.
  • TX: Abbreviation for Transmitter or Transmitting.

docs.title: U
docs.text:
• UHF (Ultra High Frequency): Ultra high frequency. The radio frequency range from 300 MHz to 3 GHz.
  • USB (Upper Sideband): Single-sideband modulation. The upper sideband of an AM signal.
  • UTC: Coordinated Universal Time. The primary time standard by which the world regulates clocks and time.

docs.title: V
docs.text:
• VFO (Variable Frequency Oscillator): Variable-frequency oscillator. A component that allows continuous tuning of the frequency.
  • VHF (Very High Frequency): Very high frequency. The radio frequency range from 30 to 300 MHz.
  • VOX (Voice Operated Exchange): Voice-operated switch. A switch that operates when sound is detected.

docs.title: W
docs.text:
• WARC: WARC bands. Amateur radio bands allocated in 1979 (12m, 17m, 30m).
  • WPM (Words Per Minute): Words per minute. A measure of telegraphy speed.

docs.title: X
docs.text: • XYL (Ex-Young Lady): Slang for the wife of a ham.

docs.title: Y
docs.text: • YL (Young Lady): Slang for a female ham operator.

docs.title: Z
docs.text: • Z (Zulu Time): Zulu time. Military/Aviation designator for UTC.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: The primary reason for using the phonetic alphabet is reliable communication. In severe and difficult situations (noise, interference - QRM, and natural atmospheric static - QRN), words and letters may sound completely different or similar to one another (e.g., "B" and "V", or "M" and "N"). Substituting a whole internationally standardized word representing each individual character guarantees unambiguous understanding across the radio network.

docs.title: Complete Alphabet Table
docs.text: Letter NATO Word Pronunciation Example in HAM Radio A Alpha AL-FA Alpha for "A" in the callsign B Bravo BRA-VO CQ CQ Bravo... C Charlie CHAR-LEE ... D Delta DEL-TA DX = Delta X-ray E Echo ECH-O ... F Foxtrot FOX-TROT FT8 = Foxtrot Tango Eight G Golf GOLF ... H Hotel HO-TEL ... I India IN-DEE-A ... J Juliet JEW-LEE-ET ... K Kilo KEE-LO ... L Lima LEE-MA ... M Mike MIKE Microphone = Mike N November NO-VEM-BER ... O Oscar OS-CAR ... P Papa PAH-PAH ... Q Quebec KEH-BECK QSL = Quebec Sierra Lima R Romeo RO-ME-O Rx = Romeo X-ray S Sierra SEE-AIR-AH SSB = Sierra Sierra Bravo T Tango TANG-GO Tx = Tango X-ray U Uniform YOU-NEE-FORM ... V Victor VIK-TAH VHF = Victor Hotel Foxtrot W Whiskey WISS-KEY W = Watts (Whiskey) X X-ray ECKS-RAY ... Y Yankee YANG-KEY Yagi = Yankee Alpha... Z Zulu ZOO-LOO UTC time is Zulu

docs.title: Numbers in Radio Traffic
docs.text:
Numbers are typically read normally in English, but with exceptions clarified here for audibility.

Number Standard Radio Pronunciation Note 0 Zero Do not use "Oh". 1 One — 2 Two — 3 Three — 4 Four — 5 Fife Pronounced as Fife to avoid confusion with "Nine" 6 Six — 7 Seven — 8 Eight — 9 Niner Pronounced as Niner to avoid confusion with "Five / Fife"

Fife and Niner

Pay close attention to these two words and use them automatically. Because of "5" and "9" in standard RS reports (Readability and Signal), these are the two most important words among numbers.

docs.title: Spelling Examples
docs.text:
Spelling Callsigns: - 9A1ZG → "Niner Alpha One Zulu Golf" - W1AW → "Whiskey One Alpha Whiskey"

Spelling in a QSO: - "My name is JASMIN, Juliet Alpha Sierra Mike India November, over."

docs.title: Prosigns (CW and Voice)
docs.text:
Procedural signals (prosigns) shorten transmission time and clarify intentions.

Prosign CW Characters Voice Equivalent When to use AR .-.-. "End of message" Used when the message is done, calling for a direct reply back. SK ...-.- "Silent Key" / "End of work" Complete QSO. End of work, silence. BK -...-.- "Break, over" Fast pause between inputs for the other station to jump in. K -.- "Over" Calling literally everyone or returning the mic. KN -.--. "Over to named only" Exclusive call; only the named station has priority to answer. DN -..-. "Do not answer" Broadcasts or CQ messages testing propagation without wanting a reply.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Saying V as in Victor instead of just Victor. This is a ← HUGE MISTAKE!.
  • The correct delivery is: "My callsign is Niner Alpha" and NEVER "My callsign is nine, letter A as in Alpha". Do NOT drag the spelling.
  • Do not use creative replacements (like "Zanzibar" instead of "Zulu" or "Xylophone" instead of "X-ray"). Zulu and X-Ray are mathematically proven to be distinct against noise.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: In modern amateur radio, the computer has become an inseparable part of the amateur radio station (shack). From logging contacts and managing digital modes to Software Defined Radios (SDR), software is just as critical as the transceiver or antenna itself.

docs.title: How to use this list
docs.text:
This comprehensive list covers numerous tools, but we recommend focusing on the basics first. Here is the "Starter Pack": - WSJT-X: The absolute standard for FT8/FT4 digital modes. - Log4OM v2: A highly capable and free Windows application with excellent integration, arguably the best general logging tool. - MMANA-GAL: One of the best beginner tools for designing and modeling shortwave wire antennas. - HamAlert: A crucial mobile tool for tracking propagation and spots. - LCWO.net: The best web tool for mastering telegraphy (CW).

Recommended list for beginners

By downloading this "Starter pack" you will cover most of the requirements to start operating on frequencies, maintain a logbook, and test your first digital modes. Coupled with mastering telegraphy via LCWO, you have a great start!

docs.title: Logging
docs.text: The logbook is the central software of every station, ensuring the safe and organized storage of contacts. * N1MM+: Leading world logger for contesting. (Win / Free) * Log4OM v2: Complete and modern general logger. (Win / Free) * CloudLog: Excellent web-based self-hosted logger. (Web / Free) * Ham Radio Deluxe: "All-in-one" system for rig control and logging. (Win / Paid)

docs.title: Digital Modes
docs.text: Digital modes have changed HF communications, enabling stable links even in poor propagation. * WSJT-X: Dominant tool for FT8, FT4, WSPR. (Win/Mac/Linux / Free) * fldigi: Foundation software for legacy modes like PSK31, RTTY. (Win/Mac/Linux / Free) * JS8Call: Allows free text utilizing the robust FT8 routing. (Win/Mac/Linux / Free) * Winlink Express: Standard for transferring e-mail messages via RF without internet. (Win / Free)

docs.title: SDR (Software Defined Radio)
docs.text: Applications responsible for processing uncommonly large bands of RF signals. * SDR#: Popular and versatile viewer for RTL-SDR dongles. (Win / Free) * SDRuno: Main software for SDRPlay receivers. (Win / Free) * Gqrx: GNU Radio-based application excelling on Linux. (Mac/Linux / Free)

docs.title: Antenna Modeling
docs.text: Designing your own antennas and predicting their behavior. * MMANA-GAL: Golden free standard for beginners. (Win / Free) * 4NEC2: Advanced NEC2-based simulator. (Win / Free)

docs.title: Propagation Tracking
docs.text: Applications for tracking solar cycles and impacts on the ionosphere. * VOACAP: Point-to-point prediction of stable radio communications. (Web / Free) * HamClock: All-in-one display for tracking satellites, solar data, and spots. (Linux/Web/Pi / Free) * SolarHam: Central site for evaluating solar flares and K-indices. (Web / Free)

docs.title: CW Practice (Learning Telegraphy)
docs.text:
• LCWO.net: Standard for online learning using the effective Koch Method. (Web / Free)
  • Just Learn Morse: Desktop tool utilizing auditory templates. (Win / Free)

docs.title: Radio Control (CAT)
docs.text: Intermediate library layers to communicate directly with hardware. * Hamlib: Open-source foundation covering over 200 modern devices. (Win/Mac/Linux / Free) * OmniRig: Highly popular Windows COM interface recognizing multiple VFOs. (Win / Free)

docs.title: Mobile Applications
docs.text:
• HamAlert: Notifications for spotting specific DX. (iOS/Android / Free)
  • RepeaterBook: Offline repository of repeater locations globally. (iOS/Android / Free)
  • APRS.fi: Visualizing user movements on the digital-location APRS system. (iOS/Android / Free)
  • SOTA Spotter: Tracking mobile station activations on mountain summits. (iOS/Android)

docs.title: Technical
docs.text: The technical aspect is the core of amateur radio. Here we cover everything from the basics of electronics to advanced communication systems.

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• Basics of Electronics: Resistors, capacitors, inductors, semiconductors.

docs.title: Antennas & Feedlines
docs.text: An antenna dictates the true nature of propagation. Placing an awful stick upon a thousand dollar transceiver easily underperforms deploying a masterfully designed aerial loop fixed upon a cheap radio station.

docs.title: Dipole (Center-Fed)
docs.text: Simplest wire iteration utilizing sheer symmetrical radiation efficiency. * Normally spanning lengths of EXACTLY \(\lambda/2\) (half-wavelength rules) of the required operational frequency scope. * Cutting at exactly the mid-point splits elements symmetrically injecting feeds via Baluns.

docs.title: Yagi-Uda (Beam Director)
docs.text: A highly directed aluminum arrow focusing all radio-electric emissions straightforward acting similarly to flashlight lenses ignoring backside strays. * Embraces a dipole element flanked actively utilizing a backing reflector pushing the element outwards via forward Directors.

docs.title: Ground Plane Verticals
docs.text: Operating flawlessly upon omnidirectional requirements (sweeping fully around 360 orientations). * Typically necessitates heavy implementation of grounding counter-poises "Radials".

docs.title: Emission Polarization
docs.text: Matching operational lines correctly maintains stability averting heavy \(20+\) dB power drops. Shooting a horizontal line while a receiver expects a vertical intercept yields dead connections. Therefore mobile operators fixant solely upon verticals whereas massive HF DX deployments align mostly horizontal bounds ignoring ambient noise pickups!

docs.title: Gain Expectations
docs.text: Always note: Antennas DO NOT produce internal free powers nor amplify signals. Gain strictly specifies that power diverted via shape lenses towards exclusive points inherently starves rear paths! * dBi: Theoretical sphere (falsely inflating marketing scopes). * dBd: Real gain measured physically opposed to a raw classic dipole shape. (\(0 \text{ dBd} = 2.15 \text{ dBi}\))

docs.title: Standing Wave Ratios (SWR) Mismatches
docs.text: Pushing 50 Ohm (\(50 \Omega\)) limits represents absolute stability. Generating incorrectly adjusted un-resonant antennas drastically forces output paths crashing out, repelling heavily sending dangerous thermal blasts straight inwardly backwards down to transceiver bounds marking a bad Standing Wave Ratio exceeding 2:1 margins!

docs.title: Specialized Space Constraints & Stealth Loops
docs.text: If lacking acres of territory these specific types allow urban apartments deployments: 1. End-Fed Half Waves (EFHW): Driving wires attaching solely using one extreme baseline without demanding center feed breaks natively pushing insane impedances exceeding 2000\(\Omega\), relying entirely upon heavy impedance 49:1 massive Unun structures. 2. QUAD serial Loops: Circular/rectangled wire elements highly resistive targeting hostile noisy atmospheric rains! 3. Small Magnetic Transmitting Loops: Heavy copper circle employing a capacitor allowing incredibly short but ultra-filtered low-noise results natively! 4. J-Poles (Slim Jims): Bent tubular J structures ideal replacing missing ground-radial planes yielding fantastic gain values upon small balconies hitting UHF and VHF requirements transparently!

docs.title: Balanced versus Unbalanced Feedpoints (Baluns)
docs.text:
Hooking an unbalanced tube line (a Coaxial output shielding mechanism) straight pushing into an exact symmetrical half dipole structure breaks its symmetry creating stray currents flowing backwards upon outer shields! Using Balanced-to-unbalanced transformational ring cores (Balun systems) or asymmetrical Ununs dictates the safe flow resolving mismatch impedances.

Reliable Cable Types Attenuation Drop (Measured 100m lines) running at 50 MHz VHF Drops on 144 MHz Extreme 432 MHz Losses Standard raw RG-58 Colossal 10 dB deficit losses! Yields a tragic ~18.5 dB power wipe! Horrific 35 decibels; practically blocking transmission! Stronger Thicker RG-213 Sustainable 4.5 dB drops. Manageable 8.8 dB transmission falloff Signals dwindle noticeably past 16dB losses. Specialized LMR-400 Minuscule ~ 2.8 dB losses.. Pristine 4.8 dB retention allowing top power! Notable but totally workable drops. Excellent build grade!

Connectors

For anything upon HF and VHF simply stick heavily attaching raw PL259 UHF caps. Extremely tough and cheap avoiding water leaks efficiently compared natively utilizing precise tiny BNC structures.

docs.title: Electronics Fundamentals
docs.text: Understanding electronics is the foundation for every radio amateur. Without knowing the basic components and laws, it's hard to understand why an antenna needs to be a certain length, what 50 Ω impedance means, or how a radio transmits a signal.

docs.title: Resistor
docs.text:
Limits the flow of electric current. Think of it as a narrowing in a water pipe.

  • Unit: Ohm (\(\Omega\)), Symbol: \(R\)
  • Uses: Voltage division, LED current limiting, pull-up/pull-down

docs.title: Capacitor
docs.text:
Stores electrical energy in an electric field between two plates.

  • Unit: Farad (\(F\)), typically \(\mu F\), \(nF\), \(pF\). Symbol: \(C\)
  • Uses: Filtering, DC blocking, tuned circuits

docs.title: Inductor
docs.text:
Stores energy in a magnetic field. Opposes changes in current.

  • Unit: Henry (\(H\)), typically \(\mu H\), \(mH\). Symbol: \(L\)
  • Uses: RF chokes, filters, transformers, baluns

docs.title: Semiconductors
docs.text:
Diode — One-way valve for current. Conducts only from anode to cathode.

Zener Diode — Conducts in reverse above a specific "Zener voltage". Used for voltage regulation.

BJT Transistor — Current-controlled amplifier/switch with Base, Collector, Emitter leads. Gain factor: \(\beta\) (hFE).

MOSFET — Voltage-controlled switch/amplifier with Gate, Drain, Source leads. Nearly all modern RF power amplifiers use RF MOSFETs.

docs.title: Ohm's Law
docs.text:
\[ I = \frac{U}{R} \]

Power: $$ P = U \cdot I = I^2 \cdot R = \frac{U^2}{R} $$

docs.title: Series and Parallel Circuits
docs.text:
Resistors in series: \(R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + \ldots\)

Resistors in parallel: \(\frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \ldots\)

Capacitors work in reverse: they add in parallel, divide in series.

docs.title: AC vs DC
docs.text:
DC = electrons flow in one direction (batteries, power supplies). AC = electrons alternate direction sinusoidally (mains power, RF signals).

Parameter Description Formula Frequency (\(f\)) Cycles per second (Hz) \(f = 1/T\) Period (\(T\)) Duration of one cycle \(T = 1/f\) RMS Voltage Effective value equivalent to DC heating \(U_{RMS} = U_{peak}/\sqrt{2}\)

docs.title: Reactance and Impedance
docs.text:
Capacitive reactance (opposes low frequencies, passes high): $$ X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} $$

Inductive reactance (opposes high frequencies, passes low): $$ X_L = 2\pi f L $$

Impedance (total AC opposition): $$ Z = R + jX \quad \Rightarrow \quad |Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} $$

The 50 Ω standard in amateur radio is a compromise that minimises cable losses while maximising power transfer.

docs.title: LC Resonant Circuit
docs.text:
When \(X_L = X_C\), the circuit resonates. Thomson's formula:

\[ f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} \]

Q factor — sharpness of resonance: $$ Q = \frac{f_r}{\Delta f} = \frac{X_L}{R} $$

Higher Q = narrower bandwidth = more selective filter.

docs.title: Transformers
docs.text:
\[ \frac{U_1}{U_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} \qquad U_1 I_1 = U_2 I_2 \]

RF transformers (balun/unun) convert between balanced and unbalanced lines. Essential for antennas — see Antennas.

docs.title: Grounding & Lightning Protections
docs.text: One of the most misunderstood and poorly implemented topics in amateur radio is grounding. Unlike simple household circuits, a radio shack requires addressing three fundamentally different grounding scopes simultaneously. Merging them wildly guarantees dangerous hazards or destroying signal integrities completely.

docs.title: 1. The Operational RF (Radio Frequency) Ground
docs.text: Serves purely one structural goal: providing the "missing opposing counter-reflection half" exclusively resolving antenna transmissions pushing return frequencies maintaining low SWR matching without burning equipment or sending RF waves stinging operators via mic interference issues. Ground rods planted specifically to save you from RF will NOT save you from 220V shocks or weather phenomenons!

docs.title: 2. The Internal Safety Protective Ground (PE limits)
docs.text: Regulated rigidly by EU safety blocks guaranteeing domestic green/yellow house plugs strictly manage dropping huge current spikes if an active raw 220 AC block wire directly burns touching external metal equipment casing, allowing safe flow instantly destroying internal fuses saving humans from experiencing cardiac arrests via touching objects!

docs.title: 3. Extremely Destructive Weather Strike Mitigations (Lightning Protections)
docs.text: Direct, unmitigated extreme path lines implementing massive thick exterior stakes buried multiple meters attempting desperately preventing millions of atmospheric Volt/Ampere surges destroying indoor operations by shooting intense strikes rapidly downwards far away returning it natively ignoring localized room electronics!

docs.title: Establishing the S.P.G. Rule (Single Point Ground)
docs.text:
How to safely route lightning away from discovering active wall sockets entering back inside computer systems looping around and blowing networking switches? - The SPG Structure: Resolving safety boundaries completely mandates establishing an isolated Single Point Ground Block (Usually using massive copper busbars / rails). EVERY isolated independent grounding system spanning the operating desks routes explicitly tightly attached reaching one sole point directly prior leaving the perimeter bridging outside. - Following routes down: The heavy exterior wire attaches passing outside plunging towards immense copper stakes buried safely in deep damp outdoor dirt. - Why this works: During phenomenal strike jolts hitting directly raising localized external values hitting 5,000V upon all items... Because everything binds directly attaching the same strict busbar plate, items inside exhibit absolutely ZERO local internal voltage differences leaving all hardware perfectly intact preventing burnouts across ports!

Preventing Chaotic Ground Loops

Tethering computer towers attached towards distinct localized western power outlets whilst keeping radio power plugged separately spanning opposite wall domains whilst physically bridging serial USBs causes destructive Ground Loops! This loop magnetically hoovers surrounding hums generating intense 50Hz audio shrieks disrupting signals due to differing internal potentials! Link everything natively targeting central Single SPG lines preventing rings!

docs.title: Outdoor Verticals Relying upon Extensive Radials planes
docs.text: Sticking a simple vertical monopole whip standing cleanly upwards ignores basic physics yielding abysmal properties wasting powers shooting energies generating heat underground natively! - Vertical bounds desperately demand arrays composed utilizing vast Radial networks deployed horizontally beneath / over grass limits explicitly forming symmetrical RF mirrors supporting the missing opposing bottom dipole half! - Throwing 16+ extensive lengths measuring perfectly ~ quarter \(\lambda/4\) properties boosts vertical capabilities enormously jumping operational outputs yielding functional DX connections skipping massive losses. - Lacking grass terrains explicitly mandates hooking Counterpoises, simple non-resonating wires dropped randomly replacing active earth ground planes simulating counter-electrical environments granting handheld / portable activations stable output paths.

docs.title: Active Protections Managing Lightning Direct Hits
docs.text:
Even implementing perfect pure ground lines grants zero abilities physically separating sky-driven charges attempting to obliterate transceiver equipment actively traveling inbound bypassing lines!

  • Implementing Protections: Line arrestors utilizing specialized combinations including GDT (Gas Discharge Tubes) natively shunt intense voltages popping links immediately dumping potentials. NEVER place inside targeting directly beside desks! They drop massive arcs generating fire hazards prioritizing placement directly outside structural limits prior passing walls connecting directly to the main massive external SPG sink paths ensuring short fast drops safely downwards maintaining extreme boundaries holding active charges away from users!

Arrestors WILL NOT Save Direct Transceiver Lines!

No module blocks million Volt physical strikes piercing raw sky systems. Relying fully holding faith assuming filters protect operations grants complete false securities during storm clusters. The ONE singular 100% physically guaranteed protection model insists entirely physically extracting thick coaxial screw caps dropping separated cables far upon grounds natively severing path continuity preventing arcing immediately prioritizing pulling plugs before localized storm warnings initiate activity.

docs.title: Hamnet (High Speed Multimedia)
docs.text: Hamnet is a high-speed amateur radio network based on the IP protocol, using modified WiFi equipment on amateur radio frequencies.

docs.title: Intro & Infrastructure
docs.text: What is Hamnet, how it works, and what equipment is used (Ubiquiti, Mikrotik).

docs.title: German Hamnet (DAeC)
docs.text: Germany has one of the most developed Hamnet networks in the world. Here we will share their experiences and best practices.

docs.title: Applications
docs.text:
• Repeater linking
  • Video surveillance
  • VoIP telephony
  • Access to servers and services within the network

docs.title: Modulation and Demodulation Concepts
docs.text: This chapter establishes a primary understanding of RF carriers and techniques of altering their paths (Modulating them) to pass human voices or PC-driven digital models into space. Firing a bare low-frequency raw vocal sound onto antennas is completely inadequate requiring impossibly massive kilometer-long antenna constructs lacking penetration. Enter the "Carrier" signal! It represents a high-frequency vehicle speeding rapidly without data until you load (Modulate) active audio directly upon it.

docs.title: Amplitude Mismatches via AM Modulation
docs.text:
• Principals: While the carrier frequency keeps a perfectly constant wavelength, it alters wildly shrinking and growing linearly reacting with the exact microphone acoustic pressures injected.
  • Spectrum Realities: It blasts three massive peaks spanning extreme sections holding upper and lower redundant sections including gigantic wasteful massive central pillars.
  • Constraints: Hugely wasteful draining power efficiency needing minimum band ranges \(6 \text{ to } 8 \text{ kHz }\) heavily subjected pulling surrounding storm statics/noise historically remaining prevalent only via Aeronautical aviation.

docs.title: Altered Frequency Shifts defining the FM standard
docs.text:
• Principals: FM structures completely drop amplitude fluctuations utilizing purely static power emissions whilst generating incredibly rapid minor left/right deviation frequency shifts responding perfectly transferring voice.
  • Deviation metrics: Re-scaling demands generally pushing narrower bounds \(\pm 5 \text{ kHz}\) upon local repeater applications whilst demanding massive broad footprints running commercial radiostations.
  • Significant "Capture Effect": A phenomenal FM trait allowing receiver decoders inherently discarding drastically weaker overlapping interferences producing extreme crystal silence when operating suppressing secondary noise!

docs.title: Single Side Band Phenomenal Efficiency (SSB bounds)
docs.text: Why push wasted redundant waves via AM structures? By slicing totally useless central carrier powers preserving only a solitary side mirror holding the vocal prints you achieve: Single Side Band (SSB). - Efficiency Results: Phenomenal 3x signal concentration forcing full 100W peaks completely inside audio bursts operating incredibly densely upon mere 2.7 kHz wide sections creating DX empires covering globally distant contacts reliably. - Band Plan Standardizing: Lower Side Bands (LSB) universally command bottom portions beneath 10 MHz operations (80m/40m). Anything sitting cleanly above strictly applies the Upper Side Bands (USB) dictating conventions.

docs.title: Continuous Wave Supremacy (CW / Telegraphy)
docs.text:
• The OOK Process (On-Off Keying): Pushing literally the simplest, cheapest and most primitive digital form: Striking an active carrier simply powering it "On" pushing tone pulses followed explicitly breaking dropping lines into absolute zeroes.
  • Power advantages: Without broad voice prints spreading bounds, CW concentrates laser-focused massive power punches pushing insanely concentrated energy crossing merely 100 Hz to 500 Hz. It produces massive \(15+\) dB increases over generic SSB operations completely piercing atmospheric noise floors enabling massive QRP portable contacts extending around the globe using sub 10W outputs!

docs.title: Digital Integrations
docs.text:
• FSK Models (Frequency Shift Keying): Creating two distinct tone patterns jumping distinct "Spaces / Marks" transmitting extremely reliable RTTY data streams.
  • AFSK Structures: Avoiding native frequency hardware jumps, these modes utilize simple soundcard audio tones feeding identically directly inside SSB voice lines letting PC modems like WSJT-X translate FT8 or classical PSK streams independently.
  • BPSK/QPSK Metrics (Phase shifting): Relying explicitly on sharp angular phase changes (e.g. 180-degree jumps holding PSK31) decoding fast binary values maintaining heavily robust protections despite extremely terrible atmospheric statics holding signals decoding values up to incredible -24dB SNR ratios!
